coredata.dc:title: Efficient photocatalytic degradation of ibuprofen in aqueous solution using novel visible-light responsive graphene quantum dot/AgVO3 nanoribbons
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, Zhen-dong
Wang, Jia-jun
Wang, Liang
Yang, Xiong-yu
Xu, Gang
Tang, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Single crystalline, non-toxicity, and long-term stability graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were modified onto the AgVO3 nanoribbons by a facile hydrothermal and sintering technique which constructs a unique heterojunction photocatalyst. Characterization results indicate that GQDs are well dispersed on the surface of AgVO3 nanoribbons and GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions are formed, which can greatly promote the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs under visible light irradiation. By taking advantage of this feature, the GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions exhibit considerable improvement on the photocatalytic activities for the degradation of ibuprofen (IBP) under visible light irradiation as compared to pure AgVO3. The photocatalytic activity of GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions is relevant with GQD ratio and the optimal activity is obtained at 3wt% with the highest separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Integrating the physicochemical and photocatalytic properties, the factors controlling the photocatalytic activity of GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions are discussed in detail. Moreover, potential photocatalytic degradation mechanisms of IBP via GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions under visible light are proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
AgVO3 nanoribbons
Heterojunction photocatalyst
Ibuprofen
Degradation pathways

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structural characterization of GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions
Mechanism of the degradation of IBP with GQD/AgVO3

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Sail Plan of Shanghai for Youth
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University
Major State Basic Research Development Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Major State Basic Research Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has been supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41573096 ), Sail Plan of Shanghai for Youth (No. 16YF1404400), the Major State Basic Research Development Program of China (No. 2011CB933402 ), Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT13078). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM (a) and TEM (b) images of GQDs (HRTEM of GQDs inset in b). Low magnification (c) and high magnification (d) SEM images of 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions. TEM (e) and HRTEM (f) images of 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions.
XRD (a), Raman (b) and FT-IR (c) spectra of pure AgVO3 and 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions.
Survey XPS spectrum (a), C 1s spectrum (b), Ag 3d spectrum (c), and O 1s and V 2p spectrum (d) of 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQD solution. (Inset: the photographs of the GQD aqueous solution under visible light and 365nm UV light.) (b) Excitation-dependent PL spectra of colloidal GQDs in water. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of AgVO3 and GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions with different contents of GQDs. (d) The photographs of pure AgVO3 and GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions.

(a) Visible-light photocatalytic degradation rate of IBP by pure AgVO3 nanoribbons and GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions with various contents of GQDs. (b) The plots of −ln(C/C 0) versus t. C 0 is the initial concentration of IBP before visible light irradiation and C is the concentration at irradiation time t. (c) TOC spectra of IBP degradation by 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions. (d) Cycling stability experiments of IBP by 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions.
CV curves of GQDs (a) and AgVO3 nanoribbons (b).

(a) Diagrams of energy position and photogenerated electron-hole pairs transfer of GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions under visible light irradiation. (b) kapp values of 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 photocatalyst with different scavenges under visible-light irradiation.
Reaction by product formation from the photocatalytic degradation of IBP with the presence of GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions.
Proposed photocatalytic degradation pathway of IBP with the presence of GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions.

head.ce:title: Efficient photocatalytic degradation of ibuprofen in aqueous solution using novel visible-light responsive graphene quantum dot/AgVO3 nanoribbons

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Institute of Applied Radiation, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Co-first authors and contributed equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Single crystalline, non-toxicity, and long-term stability graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were modified onto the AgVO3 nanoribbons by a facile hydrothermal and sintering technique which constructs a unique heterojunction photocatalyst. Characterization results indicate that GQDs are well dispersed on the surface of AgVO3 nanoribbons and GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions are formed, which can greatly promote the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs under visible light irradiation. By taking advantage of this feature, the GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions exhibit considerable improvement on the photocatalytic activities for the degradation of ibuprofen (IBP) under visible light irradiation as compared to pure AgVO3. The photocatalytic activity of GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions is relevant with GQD ratio and the optimal activity is obtained at 3wt% with the highest separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Integrating the physicochemical and photocatalytic properties, the factors controlling the photocatalytic activity of GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions are discussed in detail. Moreover, potential photocatalytic degradation mechanisms of IBP via GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions under visible light are proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: Along with the discovery of semiconducting oxide nanoribbons, a variety of inorganic materials of one-dimensional materials have been synthesized and characterized because of their novel physical and chemical properties differ greatly from their bulk counterparts [1–3]. The self-assembled structures from these nanoscale building blocks have been researched intensively and potential applications in sensors [4], photocatalyst [2,3], and lithium ion batteries [5], and so forth.

More recently, AgVO3 nanoribbons, as a new promising layered material for photocatalytic energy conversion and environment remediation, have attracted considerable scientific attention for their use in photocatalysis water splitting into H2 and O2 due to their narrow band gap [3,6–10]. However, as a photocatalyst, the AgVO3 nanoribbons suffered severe constraints in practical applications due to the bottleneck of their poor capability of separating electron-hole pairs in the photocatalytic reaction. Therefore, in order to effectively retarding the recombination of the pairs under abundant visible light irradiation, it is inevitably required to develop new photocatalytic system by modifying AgVO3 nanoribbons [9,10]. Up to now, many strategies have been employed in order to acquire AgVO3 materials with high photocatalytic activity, such as morphology control [11,12], surface modification [3,9], and coupling with other photocatalysts [13–15]. Among them, the application of heterojunction materials as photocatalysts was also a good method because the heterojunction itself could provide a potential driving force to reduce the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers [16]. For example, a high rate for the photocatalytic destruction of basic fuchsine dye was obtained by using the heterojunction consisting of AgVO3 and noble metal Ag [3,9]. However, such an approach consumed a large number of noble metals, and would increase the cost of wastewater treatment. Therefore, enhancing photocatalytic activity of novel AgVO3 heterojunction needs further study.

The introduction of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) provides a feasible approach to solve the problem to some extent. Different from toxic and unstable conventional semiconductor QDs, GQDs are non-toxicity, low cost, and long-term response stability against harsh chemical attacks or strong ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation [17–22]. These outstanding characteristics benefited its practical application in producing hydrogen or oxygen of water splitting and catalytic degradation of organic pollutants [22–28]. Besides, the electronic structure of GQDs is tunable in a wide energy range by changing size and functionalization at edge site [24–28], which allows for construction of GQD-AgVO3 heterojunctions with appropriate energy band matching while the bandgap of heterojunctions is tuned as lowly as possible for harvesting more solar light. To the best of our knowledge, there has been no report on the fabrication of nanostructured GQD-AgVO3 heterojunction photocatalyst with efficient electron-hole separation ability. Therefore, The AgVO3 nanoribbons modified by GQDs might produce a high-performance photocatalytic material for organic pollutant removal.

Herein, in this work, we gave a successful attempt to construct the GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions through coupling the AgVO3 nanoribbons with ultrathin zero-dimensional GQDs by a facile hydrothermal and sintering method. Notably, the GQDs and AgVO3 nanoribbons have matching band potentials to form the heterojunction. Therefore, horizontal loading GQDs onto AgVO3 may allow effective photoinduced interfacial charge transfer by taking advantage of intimate face contact. As such, the photocatalytic activity of this heterojunction was exhibit significant enhancement on the photocatalytic activities for the degradation of pharmaceutical pollutant under visible light irradiation as compared to pure AgVO3 nanoribbons. Ibuprofen (IBP), which is a widely used anti-inflammatory drug in the human treatment of fever and pain [29,30], and could influence cyclooxygenase reactions and therefore affect not only the reproduction of aquatic animals, but also the photosynthesis of aquatic plants. Although IBP is appeared at low levels in the aquatic environment, but it was frequently detected in municipal wastewater treatment plants effluents and natural waters [31–33], so we choose it as a model compound of pharmaceutical pollutant. This enhanced photocatalytic activity is discussed in detail, and the formations of intermediates were investigated. This work opens up new possibilities for the development of highly efficient and stable GQD-based heterojunction photocatalysts through a facile approach.

All the chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All the chemicals and solvents were used without further purification. AgVO3 nanoribbon was synthesized using a hydrothermal process [10]. Specifically, NH4VO3 (1.25mmol) were dissolved in 60mL of deionized water, then 1.25mmol AgNO3 was added into the solution by ultrasonication (500W, 40kHz) for 30min, then transferred into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (100mL) and heated at 180°C for 24h. The mixture was allowed to cool down to room temperature, filtrated using filter paper. The solid fractions were washed with deionized water and ethanol several times, and then dried under vacuum at 80°C.

GQDs were synthesized through an alkali-catalyzed water-phase molecular fusion method [28]. In a typical procedure, 0.5g 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was dispersed in an ammonia solution of deionized water (40mL, 0.4M) by ultrasonication (500W, 40kHz) for 2h. The suspension was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (100mL) and heated at 150°C for 10h. After cooled to room temperature, the product containing water-soluble GQDs was filtered through a 25nm microporous membrane to remove traces of insoluble impurities.

The 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 nanoribbon composites were prepared by a hydrothermal method. Typically, AgVO3 nanoribbon (0.1g) was added to 10mL of GQD solution (0.3mgml−1) with stirring for 1h at room temperature to obtain a homogeneous suspension. After that, the suspension was transferred into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (100mL) and maintained at 150°C for 4h, and dried at 80°C, followed by annealing at 250°C in N2 for 4h to avoid GQDs separating from the surface of AgVO3 nanoribbon. Similarly, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 7.0 wt%-GQD/AgVO3 nanoribbon composites were synthesized with various concentrations of GOD solutions by the procedure above, respectively. To compare with the photocatalytic activity of GQD/AgVO3, graphene sheet (GS)/AgVO3 nanoribbons were also prepared (details in SI).

Atomic force microscope (AFM) images were taken using a SPM-9600 atomic force microscope. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) observations were performed on a JEOL JEM-2010F electron microscope operating at 200kV. Scanning electronic microscopy (SEM) images were taken with Hitachi FESEM-4800. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded at room temperature on a Hitachi 3100 spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a Rigaku 18KW D/max-2550 using Cu Ka radiation. Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw in plus laser Raman spectrometer with λexc =633nm. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed on Thermo Nicolet Avatar 370 FT-IR. X-Ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) was collected using a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer.

The photodegradation of IBP was carried out by adding 0.01g of photocatalyst in 50mL of 10mgL−1 IBP solution at room temperature. A 350W Xenon lamp with a cutoff filter (λ>420nm) was used for visible light irradiation. Prior to illumination, the suspension was protected from light and magnetically stirred for 1h to reach adsorption equilibrium, then stirred under visible light all the time. A small amount of suspension (about 1mL) was taken out every 30min and filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane. The IBP in the suspension was quantified by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) at a certain time.

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed by using a conventional three-electrode system on a CHI 660D electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument, Shanghai, China), which consists of a GQD coated platinum sheet as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) as the reference electrode. CV was recorded in acetonitrile containing 0.1M TBAPF6 as the supporting electrolyte. The lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level of the GQDs was calculated according to the following equation: ELUMO =−e(Ered +4.4) (eV).

Formic acid (HCOOH) produced from IBP degradation were determined by ion chromatography (IC) (Dionex ICS1100) equipped with an IonPac As22 column (4 mM×250mM). The determination was achieved on hydrophilic anion exchange column. The eluent was mixed with 4.5mM Na2CO3 and 1.4mM NaHCO3 at a flow rate of 1.2mL/min. The injection volume was 25μL.

A HPLC system (Agilent, USA, 1200 Series) equipped with a C18 reverse phase column (4.6×150mm, 5μm) was employed for the determination of target compounds. A mixture of acetonitrile and Milli-Q water (75/25, v/v) was used as the mobile phase with a flow rate of 1mLmin−1, and the water was acidified with 0.7% acetic acid. The injection volume was 10μL. The column temperature was maintained at 303K, and the wavelength for UV detection was set at 263nm.

A triple quadrupole mass spectrometer (HPLC1260-G6460 series, Agilent) operating in negtive electrospray ionization scan mode was employed for the identification of intermediate compounds. A C18 reversed-phase column (3.0×100mm, 2.7μm) was used with the column temperature at 303K. Sample injection volume was 10μL, and the flow rate was 0.4mLmin−1. The mobile phases were acetonitrile and 0.1% aqueous acetic acid with the gradient: 0min 15%, 15min 50%, and 18min 50% acetonitrile. Transitions selected ranged from 100 to 500. Optimal instrumental source parameters were set as scan time: 400ms, gas temperature: 350°C, gas flow: 9Lmin−1, nebulizer pressure: 38 psi, sheath gas temperature: 350°C, sheath gas flow: 11Lmin−1, capillary voltage: +4000V, nozzle voltage: +1500V, cell accelerator voltage: 3V, fragmentor: 100V.

Total organic carbon (TOC) was determined using TOC/TN analyzer (Jena, Germany, Multi N/C 2100 expert) for selected samples. The selected samples were heated to 800°C to detect gas component parts and the detection method was applied with non-dispersive infra-red gas absorption spectrometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structural characterization of GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs were synthesized by an alkali-catalyzed water-phase molecular fusion method [28]. The main process involved 1,3,6-trinitropyrene in an hydrazine hydrate solution of deionized water followed by hydrothermal molecular fusion. Brown GQD suspensions are obtained directly by filtering the hydrothermal product with a 25nm microporous membrane without further dialysis or high-speed centrifugation. The size and morphology of the GQDs were examined by AFM and TEM (Fig. 1 a and b). Their thickness is distributed in the range of 1–6nm, with an average thickness of 1.9nm (Fig. S1), corresponding to an average layer number of ∼5, indicating that most GQDs is lesslayered. Their TEM image shows that the GQDs are well dispersed with uniform lateral sizes, whose average lateral size is 4.8nm (Fig. S2). As displayed in the HRTEM image (inset in Fig. 1b), it shows an obvious single-crystal structure of the GQDs, with an interplanar spacing of 0.21nm corresponding to that of graphene (100) planes. The interlayer spacing is 3.34Å, which determined by XRD (Fig. S3). The Raman spectra (Fig. S4) show a D band at 1369cm−1 and a stronger G band at 1587cm−1. The XPS spectra of the GQD were analyzed (Fig. S5), the GQDs show multipeaks of these spectra. Apart from the signals of CC (284.7eV for C1s) and NO2 (405.9eV for N1s, 533.2eV for O1s), COH (288.4eV for C1s, 531.5eV for O1s) signals are also detected. Moreover, the N1s signal of NO2 becomes very weak, and the O1s signal of NO2 is weaker than that of OH. We also note that strong signals of CNH2 (286.1eV for C1s, 399.8 and 401.7eV for N1s) are observed. Correspondingly, strong vibration bands of OH (3400cm−1), NH (3200 and 1620cm−1), CC (1590cm−1) and COH (1270cm−1) in the IR spectrum of the GQDs are detected consistently (Fig. S6). These results indicate that the surfaces of the GQDs are strongly functionalized in situ by both OH and NH2 groups, leading to their high solubility in water. The optical properties of the colloidal GQDs dispersed in aqueous solution are shown in Fig. 4a and b. The absorption onset is at ∼680nm, and thus the optical bandgap of the QDs is estimated to be 1.82eV. The QDs emit strong photoluminescence (PL) from green to yellow, is well confined, nearly irrespective of excitation wavelength. The detailed morphologies and microstructures of 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions (the highest photocatalytic activity for IBP degradation) were characterized by SEM (Fig. 1c and d) and TEM (Fig. 1e and f). The relatively low-magnification SEM image (Fig. 1c) shows an overall view of GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions with length of 1.2–4μm and width of 100–300nm. The high-magnification image (Fig. 1d) clearly shows some GQD particles adhering to the surface of the individual AgVO3 nanoribbon, suggesting that GQD nanoparticles are uniformly anchored on the nanoribbon. The energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) (Fig. S7) reveals that the signals of C, N and O as well as those of Ag and V elements. The corresponding element analysis embedded in the EDS panel illustrates that the atom ratio of Ag:V:O is 1:1:3.4 confirming the composition of AgVO3. Further details of the morphology are provided in the TEM image in Fig. 1e, the GQDs loaded on the surface of AgVO3 nanoribbon. The HRTEM showed the interplanar distances were 0.21nm is in agreement with the d-spacing of graphene (100) planes, and 0.306nm for the (501) crystal plane of AgVO3 [9,10,34], further confirming the formation of the GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions.

The XRD patterns of the prepared GQD/AgVO3 are shown in Fig. 2 a along with pure phase of AgVO3 monoclinic structure [JCPDS: 29-1154] for comparison [35,36]. No peaks from other phases can be detected. For the AgVO3 nanoribbons, one strongest characteristic peak appeared 29.8°, corresponding to the (501) diffraction plane of the pure AgVO3 nanoribbons [37]. The XRD patterns of the prepared GQD/AgVO3 nanoribbons indicate that the samples are well crystallized and the crystal phase of AgVO3 does not change with forming GQDs on AgVO3 surface. Fig. 2b demonstrates Raman spectra of the pure AgVO3 nanoribbons and GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions. The strongest peak at 886cm−1 results from the stretching vibrations of OVO or VOAg, and two obvious peaks at 808 and 733cm−1 are correspond to AgOAg and VOV bridges, respectively. These bands, along with those located at 251 and 168cm−1, are the clear signature of the AgVO3 nanoribbons [9,10]. Moreover, compared with pure AgVO3 nanoribbons, two additional peaks at 1587 and 1369cm−1 are observed for GQD/AgVO3 nanoribbon heterojunctions in Fig. 2b, corresponding to the G and D bands of GQDs, respectively.

XPS analysis was employed to determine the chemical composition and bonding configuration of the as-fabricated products. In Fig. 3 a, the C 1s and N 1s peaks should originate from GQDs, which certainly confirms the presence of GQDs on the surface of the sample. Apart from the strong signal of sp2 CC bond of graphitic carbon (284.7eV), the CNH2 (286.1eV) and COH (288.4eV) signals in Fig. 3b are also detected, which is detected on the curve of GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions. From the Ag 3d spectrum (Fig. 3c) of the GQD/AgVO3 composite, the two peaks at approximately 368.3 and 374.3eV can be assigned to the binding energies of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively. This is consistent with a previous research [38]. The 516, 524, and 529eV peaks in Fig. 3d can be assigned to V 2p3/2, V 2p1/2, and O 1s, respectively. To investigate in-depth the atomic structure of GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions, the FT-IR of AgVO3 and 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 composite were carried out. As observed in Fig. 2c, compared with the IR spectrum of AgVO3, the specific adsorption peaks of the GQD particles in the IR spectrum of 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 composite are observed. Besides the CC vibration at 1590cm−1, there is NH vibration at 1480cm−1, which is further confirmed by the vibration of COH at 1270cm−1 in the IR spectrum.

In order to investigate the alterations of optical properties induced by the GQDs, the photocatalysts were subject to analysis of UV–vis absorption spectra. UV–vis absorption spectra of AgVO3 nanoribbons in Fig. 4 c demonstrate that AgVO3 exhibit significantly optical absorption from UV light to visible light region (about 600nm), ascribed to the excitation from the AgVO3 valence band (VB) to the CB and gap energy is estimated to be 2.0eV (Fig. S8), as well as none substantial absorption band from 600 to 800nm, involving the transition of trap states of intrinsic defects within the bandgap [9,10]. Obviously, GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions exhibit strong spectral response in both the UV and the visible region. That is the effect of the narrow bandgap of GQDs (1.82eV in Fig. 4a). GQDs showed good yellow fluorescence properties under the 365nm UV light irradiation in the inset of Fig. 4a. The improved light absorption can therefore excite more photogenerated carriers from the GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions to decontaminate the pollutants, which would improve the practical application under natural solar light. Significantly, the 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 composite exhibited the highest absorption compared to other GQD/AgVO3. Further increase of GQD contents in 5 and 7 wt%-GQD/AgVO3 did not enhance the absorption, which could be attributed to the relatively larger GQD contents could cover up the surface of AgVO3 nanoribbon, hence reducing the absorption of light [39]. The colour appearances of the samples are shown in Fig. 4d, the colour of the samples was changed from orange to brown with increasing the contents of GQDs, resulting in a total absorption in the light range of 200–800nm.

Fig. 5c presents the TOC removal profiles of IBP decomposition on 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 composite. As depicted in the figure, IBP could be reduced efficiently with the catalysts in a short time. When the irradiation time reached 60min, the TOC reduction of IBP was more than 80%. Furthermore, complete mineralization of IBP was almost obtained at the irradiation time of 180min, demonstrating 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 exhibits good catalytic performances on IBP degradation.

Our 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions also exhibit good recycling ability in the photocatalytic degradation of IBP under visible light for consecutive four cycles. The stability of composite was evaluated by monitoring the (180min irradiation at every cycle). Fig. 5d clearly illustrates that no noticeable deactivation occurs in the repeat uses. The degradation rate of IBP is kept at 98% at the end of the four cycles, indicating a high photostability of 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 nanoribbons. The improved photostability is also due to the high-efficiency separation of electron-hole pairs with the existence of GQDs [40–42]. According to the data shown in Fig. 5, the addition of GQDs plays an important role in the visible-light photocatalytic degradation performance of the heterojunctions.

The photocatalytic results have shown the excellent photoactivity of the GQD doped AgVO3 nanoribbons on the degradation of IBP. To understand these enhancement effects induced by the GQDs, we measured the CV curve of GQDs and AgVO3 nanoribbons to determine their LUMO and CB, respectively. From the reduction potential (Ered) of −1.13V vs. SCE (Fig. 6 a), the LUMO energy level relative to the vacuum level is calculated to be −3.27eV using the equation in experimental section. Meanwhile, the CB energy level of AgVO3 nanoribbons is calculated to be −3.1eV in Fig. 6b. It is noted that the LUMO energy level is lower than the CB bottom of the AgVO3 nanoribbons, which indicates that the electronic transfer from the AgVO3 nanoribbons to the GQDs takes place when the AgVO3 nanoribbons are excited under visible light, as demonstrated in Fig. 7 a. It is thus expected that effective separation of photoexcited electrons from holes in the AgVO3 nanoribbons, and the fast injection of the electrons to the GQD LUMO through the heterojunctions would be realized, while highly active holes would be left on the surface of the AgVO3 nanoribbons. To determine the contributions of these reactive species to the IBP degradation, we performed IBP photocatalytic degradation experiments in the presence of scavengers to quench h+, O2− and OH during the degradation of IBP using ammonium oxalate (AO), benzoquinone (BQ), and isopropanol (IPA), respectively [43]. Compared with the absence of any scavengers, the addition of all the three scavengers suppresses the IBP decomposition rate (Fig. 7b), indicating that all the oxidative species contribute to the IBP degradation. The contributions of h+, O2− and OH to the overall MO degradation rate are thus determined to be 8.3%, 46.0%, and 45.7%, respectively. These results indicate that OH and O2− are the main oxidative species for the IBP degradation. The lowest contribution of h+ to the degradation is expected because h+ was consumed in oxidize water process for producing OH free radicals. We note that the production of the O2− species undergoes two basic processes, i.e. electron transfer from the CB of the AgVO3 to the CB of GQDs followed by trapping by O2 absorbed on the surface of GQDs. Moreover, NH2-functionalized GQDs can enhance surface absorption for O2 [42], which were absorbed more O2 on the surface of GQD/AgVO3 nanoribbon heterojunctions than pure AgVO3 nanoribbons. This fact indicates that the electron transfer process is highly effective owing to the presence of the ideal heterojunctions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the degradation of IBP with GQD/AgVO3
ce:section.ce:para: The degradation intermediates of IBP by the presence of GQD/AgVO3 nanoribbon heterojunctions under visible light irradiation alone were identified by IC and HPLC–MS/MS. From the analyses of IC, HCOOH produced in IBP degradation were detected. Fig. 8 showed the variation of the product during IBP photocatclytic degradation with increasing irradiation time. For HCOOH production, their concentration increased with the increase in irradiation time. Major degradation intermediates formed during the catalytic process were identified using the HPLC–MS/MS. Retention times and specific MS/MS data of the intermediates are summarized in Fig. S11. For example, the mass-to-charge peak [M−H]− of the IBP anion appears at m/z 205. Besides, some lower molecular mass-to-charge peaks of 177 and 132 are observed belonging to deeper oxidation products (Fig. S11). Additionally, a higher molecular mass peak at m/z 263 is observed, which could belong to IBP-OH adducts. The equal m/z differences between these peaks of 228 correspond to the sum of m/z peak 205 plus sodium. Obviously, these mass peaks belong to oxidation products of IBP-OH and reaction products of intermediates. Consequently, OH could be considered as the major contributor to the photocatalytic degradation of contaminants with visible-light-response catalysts herein. Thus, the potential degradation pathway, based on the discussion above, was proposed as Fig. 9 .

In summary, a series of GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions with different contents of GQDs have been successfully fabricated through a facile hydrothermal and sintering method. Characterization results indicate that GQDs are well dispersed on the surface of AgVO3 nanoribbons and GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions are formed, which can greatly promote the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic activity of GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions is dependent on GQD ratio and the optimized activity can be obtained at 3wt%. Most importantly, the novel supported catalyst (3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 heterojunctions) prepared in this study has excellent repeatability. The photocatalytic reactions appear to follow pseudo-first order kinetics. The excellent photocatalytic performance of the 3wt%-GQD/AgVO3 composite is attributed to the perfect interfacial contact between the two components and the photogenerated electrons in the CB of AgVO3 nanoribbons transferring to GQDs. According to the research results, a detailed degradation pathway of IBP was illustrated, suggesting that hydroxyl radicals initiated by photogenerated holes are the key factor for the breaking of the IBP benzene ring, different main byproducts (m/z: 221, 177 and 133) and small molecular organic acid (such as HCOOH) were detected. Our present work provides a new insight on developing GQD based heterojunction photocatalysts with effective charge separation for the efficient photodegradation of organic pollutants from wastewater.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41573096), Sail Plan of Shanghai for Youth (No. 16YF1404400), the Major State Basic Research Development Program of China (No. 2011CB933402), Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT13078).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2016.03.044.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
